U.S. Truflation is Low at 1.74%

The 10-year Treasury Yield Was Rejected at 4.20% Again

IWM Hit Fresh Records Pre-Market

The VIX Dipped Under 15 Post-CPI

Looking ahead, inflation data continues rolling in this week. December PPI hits tomorrow morning, with key components feeding into the PCE Price Index, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge. Then tomorrow afternoon, the Fed’s Beige Book of regional business activity offers color on year-end sentiment and trends across the country.

Also keep your eye on upcoming January data points—the Philly Fed Index and the NY Empire Manufacturing Index are released at the same time as jobless claims Thursday morning. Industrial Production and the NAHB Housing Index arrive before the long weekend.

Data Calendar

U.S. Q4 GDP likely near 3.5%

The Bottom Line

Traders were not bracing for much CPI-related volatility. Indeed, the S&P 500 (SP500) was indicated unchanged after the data hit the tape, but small-cap Russell 2000 futures notched more record highs after the 8:30 a.m. ET report. I’m watching the 4.2% level on the 10-year Treasury, while bank earnings continue rolling in.

data.title: Celestica: When Engineering Becomes The Moat
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celestica Inc. (CLS) is not a low-margin contract assembler anymore but is instead a highly embedded engineering partner for the hyperscalers who develop AI data centers. This leadership position in high-bandwidth networking, fast time-to-production, and R&D-driven moat anchors a 28%+ revenue CAGR through 2028.

Since my first coverage, CLS is up 282%, and 26% since my last. With CCS programs scaling, 50%+ hyperscaler share, and a visible pipeline into 1.6T and 3.2T platforms, execution speed and incumbency make displacement increasingly unlikely.

Celestica Targets 28%+ Revenue CAGR Through 2028

Celestica’s primary bullish attribute is its expanding role as a high-end tech and design partner for hyperscale clients building AI data centers. There is a deeper integration that secures future top line and creates a competitive barrier. Celestica’s transformation began in 2018 when it was moving away from low-complexity programs. Celestica now focuses on high-performance data center tech. The results of this directional change are seen in the outlook and segment performance.

The Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segment (I see this as the epicenter of this activity) may grow its top-line by ~40% in 2026, and CLS’s management sees a path for at least 40% growth continuing into 2027. This outlook is built on specific high-value programs. For instance, Celestica expects its High-Performance Solutions portfolio to derive ~$5 billion in topline for 2025 alone (an 80% growth YoY). This growth is tied to direct business engagements.

The technical side of these engagements forms Celestica’s upside. Celestica employs over 1,100 design engineers and is increasing its research spending by at least 50% in 2026, directed at next-gen tech for AI workloads like 800-gig and 1.6T Ethernet switches, rack-scale liquid cooling, and custom ASIC AI compute. Also, Celestica collaborated on a first-of-its-kind rack-scale liquid-cooled 1.6T networking product for a hyperscaler.

Celestica focuses on design engineering and its capability to operationalize complex production lines. As per the management, after receiving initial silicon samples, Celestica produced an operational prototype for the 1.6T switch in a matter of days, and it is a process that takes competitors weeks. This speed and technical expertise in next-gen hardware are advantages for top-line growth. Why? This technical lead translates directly into market share. In the high-bandwidth Ethernet switching industry, the total market share of Celestica’s offerings in the combined 200-gig, 400-gig, and 800-gig platforms stands at 41%.

In the market segment for customized products for hyperscalers, Celestica's share of total spending is 55%. This market lead in the highest-performance tiers is self-reinforcing. As tech cycles shorten and complexity increases (moving from 800G to 1.6T and beyond), incumbents, and new entrants may find it difficult to match Celestica’s accumulated expertise and existing client integration.

Moreover, the forward top-line pipeline appears solid based on awarded programs. The 2026 top-line forecast of $16 billion is a central growth point. It is a 31% increase from the 2025 outlook. Beyond that, a major program with a digital native client for a rack-scale custom AI system may begin mass production in 2027. As per the management, this single engagement is worth at least a few billion dollars in the first year. For me, the visibility extends further as management confirms Celestica has already won follow-on programs for next-gen silicon that ramp into 2027 and is working on 3.2T tech for 2028 and beyond.

Overall, this combination of deep engineering integration with large data center clients, a lead in next-gen AI networking tech, big market share in its niche, and a visible multi-year pipeline of awarded high-value projects forms a bullish case for stock price growth. Celestica will directly benefit from the massive CapEx in AI infrastructure as seen in its topline growth estimates (28% CAGR 2025-2028).

Celestica’s Valuation Leaves No Margin

Celestica's stock price downside risk is in a valuation that appears to be pricing in years of flawless growth. CLS stock’s current metrics indicate that the Street has already credited Celestica for the immense growth outlined in its outlook. This is a situation where the bar for performance is set exceptionally high, and it is leaving little room for operational missteps and market shifts.

Celestica’s forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is 51x against a sector median of 25.8x. Its forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 33.7x, more than double the sector median of 15.7x. The forward Price/Book ratio is 15.3x, far above the sector median of 4.8x. These multiples mean that Celestica’s stock is priced at a massive premium relative to its sector peers.

However, as revenue scales from roughly $12 billion in 2025 to over $21 billion by 2027, the forward P/S ratio naturally compresses without requiring multiple expansion. What looks expensive on today’s sales becomes far more reasonable as top-line growth compounds at 30%+ annually.

CLS's stock price does not point to a company waiting for a turnaround, but it reflects a company that is expected to deliver exceptional growth without errors. This priced-for-perfection scenario is a direct risk to the CLS stock price. The above growth story that I detailed is precisely what fuels this high valuation. But because these optimistic forecasts are already at the current stock price of $302, any failure to meet these high expectations may trigger a sharp price correction in CLS stock. A quarterly miss on topline or EPS targets, or any downward revision to outlook, may disproportionately impact CLS stock.

Furthermore, Celestica’s business model adds another layer of risk. Celestica’s profitability, gross profit margin of 12.04% against a sector median of 48.55% is of a business with a heavy manufacturing and integration component where the cost of goods sold is very high. Celestica has improved its operating margin, but the low gross margin indicates limited pricing power on the total value of the products it sells. In a high-valuation view, this is a vulnerability. A small increase in component costs or unexpected pricing pressure from its large hyperscale clients may squeeze profitability, making it harder for Celestica to meet the high earnings expectations.

Finally, Celestica’s growth is highly concentrated on the CapEx cycles of a few large hyperscale and digital native clients. In Q3, three clients accounted for 30%, 15%, and 14% of the total top line. The outlook for 2026 and 2027 is also heavily dependent on the timely ramp-up of massive new programs. Any delay in a client's data center build-out, a shift in a client's technology choice, or a decision to dual-source a previously exclusive program may hit hard on Celestica’s capability to attain its growth targets. Under the current valuations, Street may not forgive such a setback. CLS stock’s 220%+ 1Y price return may not have discounted these concentration and execution risks completely.

Takeaway

Celestica is doing well from an operational standpoint, but it is already baked into sustained and flawless execution. With high multiples, gross margins are tight, and leverage to a few hyperscale cloud clients, the buffer to get by is very small. Just an earnings miss could result in a sharp revaluation.

data.title: Kinsale: A Quality Player In E&S Insurance
data.description:
Thesis

I believe Kinsale Capital (KNSL) is a buy because it is a long-term quality compounder that is consistently profitable and well positioned in a growing E&S market. Their profitability is supported by a significantly low expense ratio, growing their book value and enabling ROE far above their mid-teens target, the industry average, and peers in and above their weight class. Their loss ratios show consistent improvement, while their expense ratios are reducing fast enough to maintain their competitive lead beyond the near term.

Residual income modeling implies that KNSL is currently trading close to intrinsic value with minor overvaluation, using conservative figures. Relative valuation metrics suggest it is overpriced but are notably cheaper compared to their historical averages. Price has fallen by about 13.5% in the past year due to concerns related to premium growth, competition, and the softening market. Given strong financials, compounding book value, and operational efficiency, the price reduction presents an opportunity to enter.

Business Snapshot

Kinsale Capital Group is a mid-sized, pure-play specialty insurance company in the US P&C industry, founded in 2009 and headquartered in Richmond, Virginia. The breakdown of their gross written premiums [GWP] for the first 9 months of 2025 was “70.1% casualty and 29.9% property,” with a focus on small- and medium-sized accounts. They are authorized to sell in "all 50 states, the District of Columbia, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands" using their network of insurance brokers.

With 2024 year-end data, we can see that their top 3 states for total premium volume were California (18.8%), Florida (15.6%), and Texas (13.4%). The GWP breakdown by business lines is Commercial (97.4%) and Personal (2.6%). Their top 3 underwriting divisions were all in their commercial lines, and the breakdown by GWP was Commercial property (24.4%), Excess Casualty (13.1%), Small Business Casualty (10.5%). These rankings seem to remain the same for 2025 when looking at quarterly reports, although no specific annual breakdown is provided yet.

Recent Changes

There are concerns related to weakening growth in their Commercial Property division, increasing competition from large standard admitted carriers, and cyclical downturn from a softening market. These reasons are largely responsible for the price decline of about 13.5% in the past year as of Jan 4th. I believe each of these reasons are legitimate concerns but will not undermine its ability to produce above-industry-average excess returns beyond the near term. I plan to cover why they have a structural advantage that enables superior profitability and are a high-quality long-term compounder.

Structurally Supported Profitability

KNSL has structural advantages that enable superior profitability in the E&S industry. Through impressive operational efficiency and their technological platforms, they can maintain strong underwriting profitability across hard and soft markets. For example, on the graph below, we can see their combined ratio is consistently below their peers, as well as the wider P&C industry, by an average of about 18.5 points for the past 10 years. This impressive performance largely stems from their low expense ratios, which come from a level of efficiency that management considers a competitive advantage. Their expense management has been making marginal improvements since 2017. This is enabled by their "proprietary technology platform," which allows them to process a large amount of business without a proportional increase in cost. This works alongside their efficient underwriting and claims adjusting.

An example would be their "freedom of rate and form" by refusing to delegate their underwriting or claims of authority to third parties, which also alleviates costs while ensuring consistent and full control of their claims management and risk selection. Altogether, this gives them the ability to garner excess margins or the leeway to cut rates in a competitive market while still maintaining high profitability. Their structural efficiency also ensures they can sustainably generate value; e.g., for the first nine months of 2025, their annualized ROE was 29.1%, which is notably higher than the industry average of about 10%, their mid-teens target, and their peers.

Combined Ratio: KNSL vs. P&C Industry

Despite this, it can be argued that their superior underwriting profitability may not be durable in the coming years due to the slowing of premium growth and growing competition from standard carriers this past year. If this continues, KNSL's dominant position in the E&S industry may be threatened. To give an example, the growth in their gross written premiums has fallen from 42.3% in 2023 to 6.9% for the first nine months of 2025. This decline, in part, comes from the decline in their largest division, Commercial Property, seeing a reduction of 15.2% in premiums in the first 9 months of 2025 compared to the prior year's period. They've admitted this slowing growth is mainly due to "rate declines and an increasingly competitive environment" as standard carriers enter the market. If these competitive conditions progress, their low expense ratios may be insufficient to maintain their strong margins. Larger carriers, through scale and their diversified books, can take price rate cuts to gain market share from KNSL. All of this would work together to erode KNSL's margins, industry lead, and ability to produce excess returns. Despite ROE being high, we may already be seeing it normalize since their annualized ROE for the first nine months of 2025 decreased to 29.1% from 32.3% in the prior year's period.

However, this argument might be overlooking that KNSLs slowing premium growth in their current largest division is more reflective of their underwriting discipline and strategy rather than a weakening competitive position. Key metrics support that management is prioritizing profitability over volume and selective risk-taking in response to price competition from bigger players entering the E&S market. For example, despite worsening growth in their largest division, their combined ratio for the first nine months of 2024, which was 77.6%, did not get notably worse; for the same period in 2025, it was 77.5%. This shows good stability, as this result was maintained despite declining GWP growth and 2.3 points from net catastrophe losses due to the Palisades Fire, which was more than the 1.7 points of net catastrophe losses in the first nine months of 2024, largely due to hurricanes and tornadoes. We even see improving growth in other areas, e.g., during the first nine months of 2025, excluding their Commercial Property division, growth in GWP had increased by 14.4% compared to the same prior period thanks to their business seeing "strong submission flow from brokers across most divisions." This growth serves as a shield against competitors fighting on price in Commercial Property and highlights another strength of KNSL.

I believe a crucial reason KNSL sees strong submission growth is because of their speed of execution, which is enabled by their tech-based proprietary systems I mentioned earlier. It lets their "underwriters reply to the majority of submissions within 24 hours," allowing brokers to close deals faster. That's in addition to KNSL focusing on small- and medium-sized accounts, which are often overlooked to begin with by larger carriers. Altogether not engaging in a race-to-the-bottom pricing war, they protect their margins while rebalancing their portfolio of policies at prices they find acceptable for the expected risk. In the soft market, this is a better approach than forcing expansion and growth, which can cause adverse selection where they accept low-quality risks to preserve volume, which in the future may cause higher losses and hurt profitability and ROE. This assumes they maintain their underwriting discipline by remaining highly selective, which we've seen over the years since they accept roughly 5-7% of business submissions despite the submissions they receive steadily growing each year.

Quality Compounder

Slowing growth and competition may make us forget that KNSL is still a high-quality compounder in this space due to being a good capital allocator. This has been expressed primarily through underwriting discipline and secondarily through their growing investment income, which has allowed them to generate ROE in the mid-twenties across both soft and hard markets, unlike some peers. For example, I calculated 3-, 5-, and 10-year CAGRs (end period 31st December 2025) for Book Value, Net Premiums Earned, Net Income and Investment Income for KNSL, ACGL, MKL, and RLI. As can be seen in the table below, KNSL consistently ranked highest on these four criteria across those periods of time, which include hard markets (3Y CAGRs) and soft markets (10Y CAGRs). Seeing superior performance across hard and soft markets, we can infer that their returns are more driven by their ability to allocate capital than cyclical tailwinds.

Key Metrics CAGRS

Note: Some MKL 3Y CAGRs had a negative base year, hence the N/AI think the outperformance on these metrics reinforces the existence of structural advantages related to underwriting discipline and structural efficiency, based on their technology and systems. Their rapidly growing investment income suggests they are effectively reinvesting their float, which will allow earnings to multiply even if we see their underwriting growth normalize in the current market. We see this reflected in the growth of their net income and book value, and this is only strengthened by living in a healthy and growing E&S market. I believe all of these imply long-run price appreciation because for insurers, their intrinsic value is largely driven by book value compounding at high ROE across cycles, which we see here.

Underwriting Performance

I'd like to compare KNSL with peers to showcase how competitive they are in terms of their underwriting ratios. I'll compare them with other specialty insurers of varying sizes, such as ACGL, RLI, and MKL, to showcase KNSL's dominance. The combined ratio is the sum of their loss ratio and expense ratio. I'd like to focus on their combined ratio and expense ratios since their loss ratios are competitive but not enough to be a difference maker among their peers. They boast industry-leading combined ratios resulting in substantial margins. These are mostly due to their low expense ratio, which is persistently below their peers and the P&C industry.

As seen on the combined ratio graph below, KNSL frequently demonstrates a lower combined ratio than its peers. For example, on the graph below, since 2021 they have maintained a sub-80% combined ratio, and the persistent gap over the period 2013-2025 highlights the existence of their competitive advantage. This has remained true across soft and hard markets despite larger peers exerting scale-related advantages. This highlights that KNSL's advantage is more process-driven than scale-driven, suggesting they are not necessarily at a disadvantage if larger peers continue to enter the E&S market. Having stronger margins than others corroborates the compounding of their book value and mid-twenties ROE.

They have also been seeing steady improvements in margin since 2020 along with peers, but lately, we can see since 2024 KNSL, along with some peers, has remained flat or increased, likely due to softening market conditions. Since long-term insurer returns are strongly driven by underwriting margin, seeing that they can outperform peers by a consistent margin across different markets supports the fact that they are a high-quality long-term compounder. Just for a bit of clarification, the reason for their massive dip in 2015 was due to their reliance on a multi-line quota share (MLQS) reinsurance agreement where they ceded a large portion of their risk to reinsurers in exchange for large chunks of their GWPs. This had the effect of drastically lowering their loss and expense ratios in the years before 2016. This ended in 2016 after their IPO; hence their combined ratio normalized to the 75-85% range.

Combined Ratio: Peer Comparison

KNSL has a structural cost advantage due to their tech-based systems that enable low expense ratios. The graph below shows us that even after they ended their MLQS reinsurance agreement, their expense ratio normalized to about 25% in 2017. This was below the industry's expense ratio of 27% in 2017. Their annualized expense ratio for 2025 is 20.56%, which is below the industry's 25.2% as of mid-year 2025 and also meaningfully below their peers. Since 2017, they've maintained this advantage with incremental gains. Recently peers have experienced higher expense ratios alongside a softening market. Despite sharing the same conditions, KNSL has remained stable. Their margins and low costs allow them to compete on price at levels some peers would find difficult to maintain for extended periods of time, in exchange for sacrificing margin. As they grow, their lead may widen due to size-related advantages, strengthening the case for their expenses not rising in proportion to their GWP due to their cost advantage. This shows how KNSL can maintain superior underwriting margins and manage their mid-20s ROE across market cycles, even as their premiums compound at rates greater than competitors, again reinforcing why they are a high-quality insurer among their peers.

Expense Ratio: Peer Comparison

Valuation

Multi-Stage Residual Income Model

A multi-stage residual income model (RIM) suggests that the mean intrinsic value per share of KNSL is $379.80. The stock price as of the 12th of Jan 2026 is $397, suggesting the stock is modestly overvalued at 4.3%, leaving them close to fair value. This presents investors with a chance to build or add to a position in a wonderful company at a roughly fair price, given its fundamentals.

Estimated Intrinsic Value Range

The intrinsic value per share is the sum of the current book value per share, the present value of residual income, and the present value of the terminal value. The residual income is the difference between net income and the minimum required return an investor expects for each forecast year. The terminal value is the sum of residual income earned in perpetuity from year 15 onwards. The book value per share is the assets minus liabilities, i.e., shareholders' equity, on a per-share basis.

An investor's required minimum return, aka cost of equity, is calculated using the CAPM formula, Ra=Rrf+[Ba x (Rm-Rrf)]. This formula uses the risk-free rate, equity risk premium, and the beta. The risk-free rate used is the US 10-year treasury yield of 4.175% as of Jan 2026. The US equity risk premium of 5, as of Sep 2025, is the extra return an investor expects for accepting the risks of equities over the risk-free asset. The beta of 1 tells us how volatile a stock is relative to the market. Greater than one means it is more volatile than the market and vice versa. If it is 1, then it moves in perfect sync with the market on average. Inputting these values gives me the cost of equity of 9.18%, which will be used to discount the residual income and terminal value to get their present values.

CAPM Inputs

I chose a persistence factor of 0.59; this is the decay rate of the excess returns. It corresponds to a narrow and stable moat, which I think reflects their underwriting discipline and operational efficiency in their space. The persistence factor does not have a significant effect on the intrinsic value. I set the dividend payout ratio to half in 5 years from now, which would be 1.89%; it also doesn't have a critical impact on the intrinsic value. Their dividend yield is not an attractive part of this investment since its 4-year average dividend yield is 0.16%, much lower than the sector median of 3.35%.

Out of these assumptions, the terminal ROE has the greatest impact on the intrinsic value estimate; I set it at the P&C industry ROE of 10%. A 10% terminal ROE means I assume ROE steadily declines from its mid-20s to 10% across a 15-year horizon. This implies that the firm's ability to create value normalizes the industry average over time. I wanted to use conservative figures to see if a mean undervaluation or fair valuation could be identified through this method. I think this is corroborated by management's authorization of a $250M share repurchase program in December 2025, suggesting it is reasonable compared to its intrinsic value

Relative Measures

Relative measures also confirm an overvaluation of the financial sector but a meaningful discount compared to historical averages. For example, their P/E GAAP (FWD) is 19.11x, which is 45% more expensive than the sector but about 43% cheaper than its 5-year average. Similarly, its P/B (FWD) is at 4.71x, which is much higher than the sector median at 1.25x but about 33% cheaper than its 5-year average. I think the market recognizes KNSL for its superior margins in the space and highly values its book. It doesn't usually trade around fair value; it has historically traded higher. So, by waiting for it to be undervalued by average insurer standards, one might miss the chance to enter when it's around fair value despite its above-average performance. Given the current pullback KNSL is experiencing, some may prefer to use dollar cost averaging (DCA) to build a position to take advantage of the pullback. To clarify, this is under the expectation that it will eventually rebound once the market realizes they are fundamentally strong despite the growth and cyclical changes.

Risks

Pricing and Competition: As we see the market softening, larger competitors are entering the E&S space and are chasing volume by competing on price. As this continues into 2026, it will challenge KNSL's growth and market share across key divisions like Commercial Property. We may see KNSL either proactively shrink to maintain margins, which I think is more likely and in line with their underwriting discipline, or sacrifice those margins if they deem it necessary to maintain a certain level of volume in key divisions.

Inflationary Pressures: Trade and tariff volatility introduce inflationary risk, which can result in adverse developments to their loss reserve adequacy. These pressures can affect claims cost estimates and have an adverse effect on reserve adequacy in the following years as well. For example, in Q1 2025, their overall "favorable development was offset in part by adverse development primarily from the 2017 through 2019 accident years."

Catalysts

Growing E&S Market: KNSL is well positioned as a pure-play specialty insurer in a growing part of the industry, which has seen some moderation in 2025. E&S has been seeing double-digit premium growth these past few years, with their participants experiencing "more favorable underwriting results and greater top-line growth" than the wider P&C industry.

Technology and Cost Advantage: KNSL's platforms function as a digital moat that enables speed and lower operating costs in their niche. This advantage has shown incremental improvements reflected in the expense ratio. This also provides leverage as they scale with incrementally increasing premiums, requiring minimal overhead for small and high-margin accounts that competitors may overlook. This cost discipline allows them to protect margins from a growing competitive landscape even as growth moderates.

Conclusion

In conclusion, KNSL's above-average ROE is enabled by their underwriting margins and operational efficiency, powered by their proprietary technology platform and freedom of rate and form. This enables them to maintain competitive loss ratios while having expense ratios much lower than peers and the P&C industry, allowing them to be substantially profitable across market cycles. This is in addition to absorbing large catastrophe losses each year, which showcases their resilience.

Residual income modelling suggests they are trading near fair prices while being at a rare discount to their historical averages. Recent concerns surrounding growth, competition, and cyclical change have resulted in a pullback despite underwriting quality remaining strong. This may present a good opportunity to build or add to a position in KNSL, such as through (DCA), to take advantage of a pullback, assuming an eventual rebound given their fundamentals and resilience.
